text: The surgeon performs posterior dissection in the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, carefully separating the mesorectal fat from the pre-sacral fascia while preserving the hypogastric nerves down to the levator ani muscle and inferior mesenteric flow.
The surgeon initiates lateral dissection on the right side, focusing on separating tissue layers and structures in the pelvic region.

The assistant provides traction on the broad ligament as the surgeon begins dissection around the round ligament, progressing along the external iliac vessels towards the infundibulopelvic ligament and the ureter.
The surgeon carefully dissects and delineates the infundibulopelvic ligament, revealing the gonadal vessels within the pelvic cavity.
The surgeon dissects and delineates the round ligament, clips it, and then transects it using an energy device, following the established dissection pattern.
The surgeon continues dissection along the surface of the external iliac vessels, carefully separating the surrounding tissue to maintain clear visibility of the vessels.
The surgeon carefully dissects along the outer surface of the external iliac vessels, ensuring precise separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon performs a clean dissection over the external iliac vessels, ensuring precise separation from surrounding tissues.
The dissection continues towards the already dissected infundibulopelvic ligament, maintaining a clear surgical field.
The surgeon clips and cuts the infundibulopelvic ligament containing the gonadal vessels using an energy device, ensuring safe transection of these structures.
The surgeon performs ureteric dissection, carefully medializing the ureters away from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon identifies the ureter as it crosses over the common iliac artery, highlighting the anatomical relationship between these structures.
The surgeon continues the uretic dissection until the uterine artery crossing over the ureter is visually identified.
The assistant adjusts traction as the surgeon begins resection of the obliterated umbilical artery, using precise dissection techniques to separate the artery from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon dissects the ureter until its origin from the internal iliac artery is visible, and identifies the uterine artery originating from the internal iliac artery.

The surgeon continues dissection along the uterine artery within the medial paravasical space, which is visually bounded by the ureter medially, the uterine vessel laterally, and the pelvic floor inferiorly.
The surgeon shifts focus to the lateral paravasical space and the obturator fossa after defining the medial paravasical space, preparing for further dissection in these anatomical regions.
The surgeon continues the resection from the surface of the external iliac vessels, meticulously removing fibrofatty tissue from the lateral pelvic wall and superiorly from the perivesical fat.
The surgeon secures any draining tributaries of the external iliac vein, ensuring they are safely ligated to prevent bleeding.
The surgeon clearly demarcates the lymph node-bearing fibrofatty tissue from the more yellowish perivesical fat, highlighting the distinct color difference between the two tissue types.
The surgeon removes fibrofatty tissue from the obturator fossa, progressively exposing the obturator nerve within the surgical field.

The surgeon continues dissection to clear lymphoidal tissue from the lateral paravesical space, which is visually bounded laterally by the external iliac vessels and the pelvic wall, and medially by branches of the internal iliac artery.
The surgeon carefully dissects in the obturator fossa, ensuring that the obturator nerve is protected from thermal injury during the procedure.
The surgeon uses a bipolar device with minimal lateral thermal spread to safely secure draining tributaries of the external iliac vein, avoiding thermal injury to surrounding structures.
The surgeon dissects the obturator artery, the only lateral branch of the internal iliac artery, within the obturator fossa, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon clips and ligates vessels deep in the pelvis to prevent inadvertent bleeding, ensuring hemostasis in the surgical field.
The surgeon removes the entire lymph canal packet from the lateral paravesical space and places it into a specimen retrieval bag for extraction.
The surgeon continues the lateral dissection, isolating the uterine artery and securing it with ligaclips and an energy device.
The surgeon performs dissection to separate the ureter from the stump of the uterine artery, using precise techniques to ensure clear separation of these anatomical structures.
The surgeon continues the dissection of the ureter, extending it to the uretrovesical junction due to suspected proximity of disease to the right ureter.
The surgeon uses an energy device in combination with cold scissors to perform the ureteric dissection, carefully avoiding thermal injury to the ureters.

The surgeon meticulously dissects the ureteric pedicle near the uterine pedicle, ensuring careful separation of the ureter from surrounding tissues, with consideration for potential ureteric resection and reimplantation over the bladder if disease is encountered.
The surgeon has resected the ureter up to the vesico-ureteric junction, ensuring it is separated from the stump of the uterine artery.
The surgeon performs necessary mobilization of the ureter, ensuring it is adequately freed from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon continues the dissection to identify the deep uterine vein, which is located beyond the previously dissected ureter.
The surgeon dissects the deep uterine vein beyond the ureter and safely clips it to prevent bleeding.
In the deep pelvic region, the right hypogastric nerve is visible running adjacent to the uterus and near the diseased tissue.
The surgeon dissects and clips the hypogastric nerve at the level of the pelvic floor, beyond the branches that supply the rectum.
The surgeon removes any remaining attachments, completing the lateral dissection in the pelvic region.
The surgeon dissects the vesico-ureteric junction away from the uterus and vagina, creating a clear separation in preparation for a future colpotomy.
The surgeon completes the lateral dissection, revealing the dissected paravascular spaces and the pararectal space within the pelvic region.
The surgeon turns the rectum to the right side, exposing the left-hand side for continued lateral dissection in the pelvic region.
The surgeon performs dissection on the left side, carefully managing the uterine pedicles, while addressing the disease predominantly located on the right side.
The surgeon dissects the infundibulopelvic ligament away from the ureter, then clips and cuts it using an energy device, ensuring safe separation of these structures.
The surgeon continues the dissection along the ureter, carefully avoiding thermal injury to the structure.
The surgeon continues dissection and enters the uterine tunnel, navigating through the anatomical spaces created during previous dissection steps.
The surgeon delineates the uterine artery, securing it between clips to prevent bleeding during the procedure.
The surgeon performs caudal dissection to expose the deep uterine vein on the left side, securing it between clips to prevent bleeding.
The surgeon proceeds with the anterior dissection after completing the left lateral dissection, focusing on separating the anterior anatomical structures.
The surgeon carefully dissects the bladder away from the anterior surface of the uterus and vagina, revealing the anatomical separation between these structures.
The surgeon meticulously defines both ureters, tracing them to the vesico-ureteric junction on each side, ensuring clear visualization and careful handling of these structures.
The surgeon demonstrates the vesico-vaginal plane between the bladder and the vagina, which serves as the plane of dissection in the ongoing procedure.
The surgeon carefully dissects along the anterior surface of the vagina and cervix, ensuring the instruments remain flush against these structures to avoid damage.
The surgeon encounters simple small blood vessels during the anterior dissection, which are visible in the surgical field.
At the vesico-ureteric junction, the surgeon prepares to control the area using clips or an energy device, ensuring precise management of the anatomical structures.
The surgeon performs a colpotomy using a McCartney's tube, ensuring both ureters remain visible throughout the procedure.
The surgeon performs an anterior colpotomy, making an incision at the anterior surface of the vagina and cervix, while maintaining visibility of the ureters.
The surgeon carefully maintains visualization of both vesico-ureteric junctions during the procedure, ensuring these anatomical structures remain visible throughout the surgical field.
During the posterior colpotomy, the surgeon carefully dissects the rectovaginal septum, maintaining close proximity to the vaginal wall to ensure precise separation.
The surgeon carefully dissects along the posterior vaginal wall, clearly delineating the rectovaginal septum while ensuring the rectal tube is safeguarded.
The surgeon dissects the vagina from the rectal tube, ensuring a sufficient length is available for future colpotomy closure and rectal stapling.
The surgeon performs a per rectal examination to visually assess and confirm the extent of the tumor within the rectal area.
The surgeon confirms a safe margin and uses an endostapler to staple off the rectal tube.
The surgeon carefully staples the rectal tube while ensuring the ureters are safeguarded, maintaining their position away from the stapling site.
The surgeon closes the vaginal vault using a barbed interlocking suture, ensuring secure approximation of the tissue edges.
The surgeon visually inspects both ureters to ensure their integrity and safety during the procedure.
The surgeon performs a stapled colorectal anastomosis using a circular stapler, ensuring the colonic conduit is correctly positioned before joining the bowel segments.
The surgeon performs an air leak test to ensure the integrity of the colorectal anastomosis after securing it with a circular stapler.
The surgeon creates a diversion loop ileostomy, forming a visible stoma on the abdominal surface.
